description: [FULL] The Christianization of Kievan Rus' was a long and complicated process. The traditional view, recorded in the Russian Primary Chronicle, dates the definitive Christianization to c. 988, when Vladimir the Great was baptized in Chersonesus and proceeded to baptize his family and people in Kiev. The process was preceded by earlier Christian influences, including the baptism of Olga around 957. Instances of pagan reaction were documented in Novgorod, Rostov Land, and Kyiv in the century after the baptism.

research_question: Насколько мирно, каким путем и почему прошло Крещение Руси?
description: [FULL] The Baptism of Vladimir I occurred in 988, marking the official adoption of Eastern Christianity as the state religion of Kievan Rus. Vladimir converted to Christianity and mandated the baptism of his subjects in the Dnieper River. His conversion was influenced by political alliances and cultural considerations, including admiration for Byzantine liturgical practices, and was contingent upon his marriage to Anna, sister of Emperor Basil II. The adoption of Orthodoxy introduced legal and cultural frameworks, though pagan practices persisted, leading to a blend of Christian and pre-Christian customs.

research_question: Насколько мирно, каким путем и почему прошло Крещение Руси?
description: [FULL] The Baptism of Rus' refers to the mass baptism of Kiev residents in 988 as Grand Prince Vladimir accepted Orthodox Christianity. Introduction of Christianity began at least a century earlier with Cyril and Methodius. As early as 867, Patriarch Photius advised that Rus' members were enthusiastic Christians, but they remained pagans. Princess Olga visited Constantinople and became an Orthodox Christian, but her son Svyatoslav remained pagan. After consulting with boyars, Vladimir dispatched envoys to study religions; they objected to Islam, Judaism, and German Christianity but praised Hagia Sophia. Concurrently, Emperor Basil II approached Vladimir for aid, and Vladimir was baptized and married Anna. He then called the people of Kiev to baptism in the Dnieper River.

description: [FULL] This note explains the role of aluminum in continuous hot-dip galvanizing. Aluminum is added not for corrosion resistance but to ensure good coating adhesion during forming. Without aluminum, brittle Fe-Zn intermetallics form, leading to poor adherence. Aluminum forms a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that controls the reaction. The note describes the metallurgical reaction, diffusion, and the importance of proper bath temperature (around 465°C) and immersion time.

description: [SNIPPET] The study characterizes Fe2Al5-xZnx inhibition layer formation in Zn baths with 0.14-0.28 wt% Al. At 0.14 wt% Al, the layer consists of fine equiaxed crystals without preferred orientation. At ≥0.18 wt% Al, oriented domains form, with epitaxial growth increasing with Al content. At 0.28 wt% Al, a single-variant crystal layer can grow epitaxially, drastically reducing Al-pickup rate and inhibition layer thickness. The inhibition layer improves wettability and inhibits brittle Fe-Zn phase formation.

description: [FULL] Zinc dross, composed of intermetallic compounds, is a significant defect in hot-dip galvanizing. Bottom dross (FeZn7) settles at the bottom, while floating dross (Fe2Al5) suspends at the top; the aluminum content determines the type. Dross causes surface defects like zinc grains, nodules, and uneven coating, and can lead to underplating. Control measures include maintaining bath aluminum at 0.18–0.22% and temperature at 460±3°C, ensuring raw material cleanliness, and regular cleaning of the furnace nose.

description: [FULL] The study investigates zinc dross removal in hot-dip galvanizing using a three-dimensional mathematical model coupled with electromagnetic field, flow field, and air-knife jet flow. research_question: На агрегате непрерывного горячего цинкования (АНГЦ) стальная полоса сначала проходит через печь термохимического отжига, где в контролируемой восстановительной атмосфере (обычно смесь водорода и азота) происходит удаление оксидов, рекристаллизация структуры и формирование требуемых механических свойств, после чего очищенная и активированная поверхность направляется в ванну с расплавленным цинком, где за счёт диффузионного взаимодействия железа и цинка формируется многослойное покрытие; далее избыточный металл снимается газовыми ножами, обеспечивая заданную толщину покрытия. description: [FULL] A neutron moisture meter utilizes neutron scattering to measure water content, most frequently in soil or rock. The technique is non-destructive and sensitive to moisture in the bulk of the material, not just the surface. Water is an effective neutron moderator due to its hydrogen content, slowing high-energy neutrons. A source of high-energy neutrons (typically a radionuclide like californium or americium-beryllium) and a detector sensitive to low-energy (thermal) neutrons are used; the detection rate is governed by the water content.

description: [FULL] The blog post compares different moisture sensor technologies. Capacitive and resistive sensors use electromagnetic waves at low frequencies to determine electrical properties, but these characteristics do not have a linear relationship with water content, decreasing the useful working range. They are also affected by salts, density, and temperature changes, making them sensitive to material variations. Microwave analogue sensors are also affected by temperature and salt changes. NIR sensors only give a surface reading and are affected by color, ambient light, and temperature. Nuclear sensors are expensive but provide very accurate measurements.

description: [FULL] The article discusses the difference between TDR and capacitance sensors for measuring soil water content. Both techniques measure the dielectric permittivity of the surrounding medium. Capacitance sensors determine dielectric permittivity by measuring the charge time of a capacitor using the medium as a dielectric. TDR sensors measure reflectance of an electrical pulse containing a wide range of frequencies. The article notes that for most scientists, a simpler and less costly capacitance sensor may be sufficient for monitoring soil water content.

description: [FULL] The page provides an overview of microwave and UHF moisture meters. These devices measure moisture by tracking differences in electrical properties of dry matter and water. A pulse passes through the analyzed material, and the weaker the microwave radiation, the lower the moisture concentration. The main advantage is non-contact measurement without damaging the material, with penetration depth up to 0.5 meters for bulk substances. description: [FULL] The page discusses the challenge of measuring moisture in magnetite iron ore concentrate due to its high conductivity and microwave absorption. Magnetite ores have conductivity 10-100 times higher than hematite and siderite ores, and microwave absorption is tens to hundreds of times greater. Standard microwave amplitude-phase analyzers cannot measure moisture in magnetite concentrates. The solution is the MICRORADAR115V13K contact moisture meter with two open quarter-wave vibrators as antennas immersed in the material, with a working base of 20-50 mm. The sensor is made of hard alloys and wear-resistant polyurethane, providing automatic temperature correction, current output, and RS-485 communication.

description: [FULL] This article describes the application of a microwave-based analyzer for online measurement of moisture content of bauxite ore on a conveyor belt. Microwaves interact with water due to its high dielectric constant, causing attenuation and phase shift, which are directly proportional to moisture content.
